items.title: fixCronField() expands bare N/step to minute range regardless of field position
items.intent: Cron expressions must be correct. A field fix that silently writes 0-59 into a day-of-month slot produces an invalid schedule that OpenClaw either rejects or runs at wrong times.
items.description: fixCronField() in generate.ts always expands bare N/step to 0-59/step. It's called via .map(fixCronField) on all 5 cron fields. A bare 5/15 in the hour field (range 0-23) becomes 0-59/15 which is out of range. The function needs the field position index to select the correct range from CRON_FIELD_RANGES.
steps: Update fixCronField() signature to accept the field index parameter
Use CRON_FIELD_RANGES[index] to compute the correct range (e.g. 0-23 for hours, 1-31 for day-of-month)
Update the .map() call site to pass the index: .map((field, i) => fixCronField(field, i))
Add test cases: bare N/step in minute field → 0-59/step, in hour field → 0-23/step, in day-of-month field → 1-31/step
Add test case: bare N/step in day-of-week field → 0-7/step
acceptanceCriteria: fixCronField('5/15', 0) returns '0-59/15' (minute field)
fixCronField('5/15', 1) returns '0-23/15' (hour field)
fixCronField('5/15', 2) returns '1-31/15' (day-of-month field)
fixCronField('5/15', 4) returns '0-7/15' (day-of-week field)
normalizeCronExpr('*/10 5/3 * * *') produces a valid 5-field expression with correct ranges
items.groomingNotes: Confirmed: fixCronField (generate.ts:845-851) hardcodes 0-59 for all fields. CRON_FIELD_RANGES (generate.ts:693-699) already defines correct ranges per position. Fix is mechanical: pass index, lookup range. Tests go in generate.test.ts alongside existing cron validation tests.

items.title: normalizeMorningBrief() silently defaults to 7am on invalid input instead of throwing
items.intent: Blueprint config errors must surface at generation time, not silently produce wrong schedules. A typo like '7:00 AM' or '07:00 UTC' must fail fast, not run the morning brief at an unexpected time.
items.description: normalizeMorningBrief() in generate.ts falls back to '0 7 * * *' when the input doesn't match HH:MM format. This silently masks blueprint errors. normalizeCronExpr() correctly throws on invalid input — normalizeMorningBrief should be consistent.
steps: Replace the fallback return '0 7 * * *' with a thrown Error that includes the invalid input string
Error message should suggest the expected HH:MM format
Add test: normalizeMorningBrief('7:00 AM') throws with descriptive message
Add test: normalizeMorningBrief('07:00 UTC') throws
Add test: normalizeMorningBrief('7h00') throws
Verify existing tests for valid inputs ('07:00', '6:30') still pass
acceptanceCriteria: normalizeMorningBrief('7:00 AM') throws an Error mentioning expected HH:MM format
normalizeMorningBrief('07:00') still returns '0 7 * * *' (valid input unchanged)
normalizeMorningBrief('23:59') still returns '59 23 * * *' (edge case valid)
TypeScript compiles cleanly
items.groomingNotes: One-line fix: replace 'return "0 7 * * *"' at generate.ts:836 with a throw. Tests go in generate.test.ts.

items.title: Ollama model loaded with native max context causing massive VRAM waste
items.intent: Compiler must cap the Ollama model's contextWindow so KV cache doesn't consume multiples of the actual need. On 2026-04-16 Clawdius's gemma4:26b loaded with 262K context, pinning ~24GB VRAM and causing the reported GPU/perf degradation.
items.description: When composition.modelContextWindow is unset, models.providers.ollama.models[] is empty, and OpenClaw auto-discovers the model's native context (262K for gemma4:26b). Ollama allocates KV cache for the full context. Fixed for gemma4:26b by setting modelContextWindow: 16384 in clawhq.yaml. Compiler should either set a sane default or warn when unset on local models.
steps: Decide on default contextWindow for auto-discovered ollama models (e.g. 16384 or 32768)
Emit that default in renderOpenclawJson when isLocal and modelContextWindow unset
Add doctor check: flag when models.providers.ollama.models[] is empty AND a local model is primary
Document modelContextWindow in clawhq.yaml reference
acceptanceCriteria: New deployments on ollama don't load the model with the full native context window
doctor surfaces the condition with actionable remediation
composition.modelContextWindow override still wins
items.groomingNotes: Partial fix shipped 2026-04-16 (modelContextWindow user-surface in clawhq.yaml + compiler emit). This item covers the default + doctor-check follow-up.

items.title: Default local model gemma4:26b is unreliable for agentic tool use
items.intent: Default local model must handle tool calls without runaway loops. gemma4:26b triggered a 10h GPU pin via a tool-call loop on 2026-04-16 (project_runaway_session_20260416).
items.description: DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL in compiler.ts is 'ollama/gemma4:26b'. doctor already flags it as unreliable for tool use via model-agentic-capable check. Default should be an agentic-capable model (qwen2.5:14b or similar) that fits comfortably in 32GB VRAM with sensible context.
steps: Evaluate candidate local models: qwen2.5:14b, qwen2.5:32b, llama3.3:70b — size, tool-call reliability, VRAM footprint
Update DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL in src/design/catalog/compiler.ts
Update model-alias-defaults if applicable
Update doctor model-agentic-capable allowlist
Migration note in clawhq update for existing gemma4:26b deployments
acceptanceCriteria: doctor model-agentic-capable passes on fresh install
Default model fits in 32GB VRAM with 32K context
Existing deployments get a migration suggestion, not an abrupt switch
items.groomingNotes: Origin: 2026-04-16 Clawdius triage. Runaway-session incident today traced to gemma4:26b tool-call loop. Doctor already has the warning — this bug is to eliminate the warning by picking a better default.

items.title: openclaw.json.clobbered backup files accumulate indefinitely
items.intent: Config write path creates .clobbered.* backups on every overwrite without cleanup. In 2026-04-16 Clawdius triage, ~/.clawhq had 20+ .clobbered files spanning 5 days, which led a concurrent Claude session to misdiagnose the deployment as actively thrashing.
items.description: Apply writes a .clobbered.TIMESTAMP backup each time openclaw.json changes. No rotation. Over time this (a) wastes disk, (b) clutters diagnostics, (c) actively misleads operators into thinking an agent is iterating.
steps: Add rotation: keep last N=5 .clobbered files, delete older
Add doctor check: warn if .clobbered file count > 5
Consider a single rolling .bak instead of timestamped chain
acceptanceCriteria: ~/.clawhq never accumulates more than 5 .clobbered files
doctor surfaces stale backups
items.groomingNotes: Discovered during 2026-04-16 GPU triage — purged manually. Low priority but a recurring source of diagnostic noise.

items.title: No instrumentation of per-turn token usage — context sizing is guesswork
items.intent: Operators can't size modelContextWindow intelligently without knowing actual token usage per turn. Today's 2026-04-16 triage picked 32K based on hardware headroom, not observed p95 — which is line-go-up VRAM utilization theater, not engineering.
items.description: OpenClaw emits logs but there's no aggregated view of prompt tokens, completion tokens, or context-fill ratio per turn. Without it: (1) operators over-allocate VRAM defensively, (2) can't detect truncation pain, (3) can't tell when session history is the bottleneck vs tool output bloat. Three levers all unlit.
steps: Identify where OpenClaw logs per-turn token counts (gateway WS frames or agent logs)
Add a clawhq logs --tokens mode or a doctor check that summarises p50/p95 input/output tokens over last N turns
Surface context-fill ratio (input_tokens / contextWindow) so operators see headroom
Recommend modelContextWindow based on observed p95 + 2x safety factor
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq surfaces observed token usage without requiring grep through raw logs
doctor suggests a data-driven modelContextWindow when current value is > 4x observed p95 (over-allocated) or < 1.5x observed p95 (at risk of truncation)
items.groomingNotes: Origin: 2026-04-16 thinking-toolkit stress test on the ctx sizing recommendation. All three attacks (VRAM scaling extrapolation, lost-in-the-middle, assumed truncation) were WOUNDED by lack of data. This bug closes that gap.

items.title: session-memory hook disabled for local models — forces unbounded history growth
items.intent: session-memory is OpenClaw's built-in conversation compaction. Disabling it for local models means every session grows monotonically until the context ceiling, which (a) wastes tokens on old content, (b) triggers truncation on long sessions, (c) promotes 'lost in the middle' attention degradation.
items.description: renderOpenclawJson disables session-memory when isLocal is true (compiler.ts around the hooks.internal block). Comment says the LLM slug generator has a hardcoded 15s timeout that large local models can't meet. That's a workaround — the real fix is a shorter/simpler slug prompt or longer timeout upstream. Current state makes large ctx windows a liability instead of an asset.
steps: Investigate the 15s slug-generation timeout — is it configurable upstream?
Option A: shorter deterministic slug (first 40 chars of first user message, no LLM call)
Option B: wire configurable slug timeout through clawhq.yaml
Option C: contribute upstream fix to OpenClaw
Re-enable session-memory for local models when whichever path lands
acceptanceCriteria: session-memory works reliably on gemma4:26b and similar local models
Long sessions compact instead of accumulating to context ceiling
No spurious 15s timeout errors in logs
items.groomingNotes: Pairs with BUG-006 — compaction lets you run a smaller modelContextWindow safely. Fixing this is higher leverage than tuning ctx size.

items.title: No continuous credential health probe — silent rot between deploys
items.intent: Credentials validated at `clawhq up` (via verify step) can go bad any time after — token regenerated, API access revoked, quota exhausted. Nothing re-probes. Operator finds out when a channel stops responding, by which time the agent has been degraded for hours or days.
items.description: On 2026-04-16 Clawdius's Telegram bot token went invalid between the morning's `clawhq up` and the afternoon. Bot auto-restarted 6+ times against 401s, spamming logs for ~30 minutes with no alert. doctor's cred-proxy-healthy check passed because the proxy was up — the token was dead server-side but the proxy didn't know. Same pattern already observed today for 1Password (401) and X (403).
steps: Add per-channel live probe to clawhq creds (not just the proxy — hit the actual provider's whoami/getMe)
Add doctor check that runs the same probes on-demand (reuse verify.ts probes)
Add an ops timer that runs creds probe every N hours and alerts on failure
Failed-credential alerts route through the agent's messaging channel if healthy, otherwise host notification
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq creds` reports live validity for every configured channel/provider
doctor surfaces the same information without manual invocation
Silent credential rot is detected within one ops cycle, not when the user notices
items.groomingNotes: Origin: 2026-04-16 Telegram outage. verify.ts already has the probe logic — needs to be runnable standalone and on a timer.

items.title: Telegram channel retries forever on permanent auth failures
items.intent: When a bot token is 401 Unauthorized, no amount of restarting will fix it. Exponential-backoff retrying a permanently-dead credential wastes resources, spams logs, and delays operator attention. The retry loop should classify 401/403 as permanent and surface the failure, not absorb it.
items.description: Today's logs showed telegram provider restarting 6/10 times at 5s, 11s, 21s, 40s, 83s, 170s backoff — every attempt failing with `GrammyError: 401 Unauthorized` on deleteWebhook/setMyCommands. The loop eventually gives up after 10 attempts but nothing escalates. Between session restarts, the counter resets — so it can retry forever without the operator learning.
steps: In OpenClaw's telegram provider (upstream), classify GrammyError 401/403 as unrecoverable
On unrecoverable: stop retrying, mark channel as failed-auth, emit a distinct event
In clawhq: doctor check that flags channels in failed-auth state
Surface in logs at ERROR level (currently mixed DEBUG/ERROR noise) and in status dashboard
acceptanceCriteria: 401/403 on Telegram API stops the retry loop after 1 attempt, not 10
Operator sees 'Telegram token invalid — regenerate via @BotFather' prominently, not buried in a retry spam stream
Other transient failures (network, 5xx) still retry with backoff
items.groomingNotes: Upstream fix preferred; clawhq-side detection is the fallback. Pairs with BUG-009.

items.title: No audit trail for credential changes — can't tell what/when clobbered a secret
items.intent: When a credential goes bad, operator can't answer: did clawhq apply overwrite it? did clawhq init reset it? did a build step touch it? was it only a provider-side revocation? Without a log, every incident becomes archaeology. User explicitly raised this concern on 2026-04-16.
items.description: clawhq writes to .env via writer.ts with credential-preservation logic (protectCredentials in apply/index.ts) — but emits no audit entry when a secret is written, preserved, or detected changed. Similarly build and init flows touch credentials without a durable record. When the .clobbered.* files accumulated earlier this week (BUG-005), there was no trace of what agent or command triggered each rewrite.
steps: Define a credential-events log (append-only JSONL at .clawhq/audit/credentials.jsonl)
Emit events from: apply writer, build compose gen, init wizard, integrate add/remove, connect
Event fields: timestamp, command, file, field, action (write|preserve|detect-changed|skip), reason
Add `clawhq audit credentials [--since=DATE]` to query the log
Redact secret values — log only field names and hash of value for change detection
acceptanceCriteria: Every credential write/preserve is recorded with source command and timestamp
`clawhq audit credentials --since=yesterday` shows what clawhq touched
No secret values ever appear in the audit log
items.groomingNotes: Origin: 2026-04-16 user question 'is our devops process clobbering config'. Today's Telegram rot was a provider-side revocation, not clawhq — but we couldn't prove that without reading .env mtime and guessing. Audit log settles the question.

items.title: Agent reads non-existent workspace files — identity or tool config drift
items.intent: Tool logs show repeated read failures for files the agent expects to exist: WATCHLISTS.json, memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md, and a path-escape attempt for /app/skills/swing-scanner/SKILL.md. Either the identity files point to stale paths, skills reference files outside the sandbox, or boot hooks aren't seeding daily memory files.
items.description: Errors observed on 2026-04-16: (1) `ENOENT ~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/2026-04-16.md` — daily memory file not being seeded; (2) `ENOENT WATCHLISTS.json` — referenced by agent/identity but not generated; (3) `Path escapes sandbox root: /app/skills/swing-scanner/SKILL.md` — skill instructing the agent to read from /app/ which is outside the workspace sandbox.
steps: Audit identity files (AGENTS.md, TOOLS.md, WATCHLISTS refs) for paths that don't get generated
Fix or remove references that escape sandbox (/app/skills/*/SKILL.md is a skill-authoring path, not a runtime read path)
If daily memory files are expected, add a boot hook or cron job to seed memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md
If WATCHLISTS.json is expected, generate it or remove the reference
acceptanceCriteria: No ENOENT read failures for files the agent is told exist
No path-escape errors in normal operation
Agent identity and skills reference only paths that exist or are optional
items.groomingNotes: Surfaced during 2026-04-16 Telegram diagnostic. Not urgent but each failed tool call burns context and may trigger model confusion / retry.

items.title: devices/paired.json not protected in apply SKIP_PATHS — future regressions could wipe pairings
items.intent: Pairing state (devices/paired.json, devices/pending.json) is critical user state — losing it means every paired device has to be re-approved. Today's audit shows clawhq source has zero explicit handling for the devices/ directory. It survives today only because the compiler happens not to emit anything there. Anyone adding a devices-related compiler output without knowing this would silently wipe approved pairings.
items.description: grep across src/ shows 'devices' mentioned only in src/config/ownership.ts (the classification table) and its test. The apply SKIP_PATHS set in src/evolve/apply/index.ts lists workspace/MEMORY.md, clawhq.yaml, credentials seeds, and docker-compose.yml — but not devices/paired.json or devices/pending.json. A future compiler change that emits a devices/* file (e.g. a template or placeholder) would get written on every apply, clobbering real pairing state.
steps: Add 'devices/paired.json' and 'devices/pending.json' to SKIP_PATHS in src/evolve/apply/index.ts
OR add a broader devices/ rule to ownership.ts marked stateful-user
Add an apply test that seeds a paired.json with a fake device and confirms it survives an apply cycle
Document the protection in ARCHITECTURE.md or a short comment on SKIP_PATHS
acceptanceCriteria: devices/paired.json is explicitly protected in code, not accidentally by omission
Regression test fails if someone removes the protection
ownership.ts deploy-unclassified check recognises these as stateful
items.groomingNotes: Prophylactic — today paired.json survives because nothing emits to devices/. This bug is the belt-and-braces fix so it keeps surviving when someone adds a devices/ compiler output without reading every code path. Complements BUG-011 (audit log) — once both land, pairing state is (a) explicitly protected and (b) any future clobbering attempt leaves a trail.

items.title: ownership.ts table incomplete — 117 deployment files unclassified
items.intent: The new deploy-unclassified doctor check (added in the ownership refactor) flags 117 files without an ownership rule. The check is useful but its companion table is a stub. Either the table gets filled out or the check stays noisy and operators learn to ignore it — which defeats both.
items.description: `doctor` on 2026-04-16 reported: 117 deployment file(s) not classified — .env, clawhq.yaml.pre-cron-fix.bak, credentials.json, engine/Dockerfile.stage1, engine/clawwall/curl-egress-wrapper, +112 more.
steps: Inventory the 117 flagged paths
Add rules to src/config/ownership.ts covering the standard generated+stateful file set
Decide per-path: compiler-owned, stateful-user, stale-backup, or platform-managed
Prune stale backups (.pre-cron-fix.bak pattern) as a separate hygiene pass
acceptanceCriteria: doctor deploy-unclassified reports 0 unclassified files on a standard deployment
ownership.ts covers every path the compiler emits plus stateful paths apply skips
items.groomingNotes: Companion to the ownership work landed earlier. Noise reduction — check is correct, table is stub.

items.title: Verbose tool outputs bloat context — no per-tool output caps
items.intent: Tools like email, news, search, quote, and market-data tools can return multi-KB payloads that flow straight into the model's context. A single tool call can consume more tokens than the entire identity + user message. Caps don't exist.
items.description: Tool scripts don't truncate or summarise output. A full email body, a news scan with 20 articles, or a market-data JSON dump all hit the model as-is. Two consequences: (a) context fills fast, driving premature truncation or large modelContextWindow allocation, (b) the model spends attention on mostly-irrelevant detail.
steps: Audit each generated tool in src/design/tools/ for output size distribution
Add a max-output-bytes default per tool (e.g. email: headers + 2KB snippet; news: titles + urls only; quote: tabular summary)
Provide a --full or --raw flag to bypass the cap when the agent explicitly needs more
Document the caps in TOOLS.md so the agent knows to re-request with --full
acceptanceCriteria: No tool's default output exceeds ~2KB without an explicit flag
Full content still accessible via explicit override
Measured per-turn token usage drops for tool-heavy sessions
items.groomingNotes: Third leg of the context-streamlining triad with BUG-006 (measure) and BUG-007 (compact). BUG-008 bounds inputs. Ship in that order — measure before trimming, compact before capping.
